Dheeran Chinnamalai

# Dheeran Chinnamalai

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

![dc](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!IBDp!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fcc7c4c62-034a-4746-9388-f4b303b6194d_410x615.jpeg "dc")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Kongu Nadu, located in the western part of Tamil Nadu and flanked by the Western Ghats, comprises the districts of Coimbatore, Tiruppur, Erode, Salem, Namakkal, Karur, Dharmapuri, and the Nilgiris. The region derives its name from a local word meaning "honey", reflecting its agricultural richness.

During the Sangam period (1st to 4th centuries CE), Kongu Nadu served as an important seat of the Chera dynasty. Geographically, it is considered the gateway to Kerala, as it forms the eastern entrance to the Palakkad Gap—a crucial mountain pass that has long served as the principal trade route between Tamil Nadu and the western coast.

The region was later ruled by the Gangas of Talakkad, followed by the Cholas during the 10th century CE, and subsequently became part of the Vijayanagar Empire. After the fall of Hampi in the wake of the Battle of Talikota (1565), control of Kongu Nadu passed to the Madurai Nayaks, who had originally served as military commanders under the Vijayanagar rulers before establishing their own independent kingdom.

In the late 18th century, the region came under the dominion of the Kingdom of Mysore. Following the death of Tipu Sultan and the fall of Srirangapatnam in 1799, Kongu Nadu was annexed by the British, becoming part of the expanding Madras Presidency.

The man who would lead the revolt in Kongu, was born as Theerthagiri on April 17, 1756, in the town of Melyapalam in Erode district, who would later become better known as Dheeran Chinnamalai. The second of six children of Rathina Swamy and Periyaatha, he had an elder brother Kulandhaisamy, and three younger brothers, Thambi, Kilothar and Kuttisamy, along with a younger sister Parvatham, in a large land-owning family of Kottravel Sarkkarai Mandraadiaar.

Rathina Swamy managed the family's extensive lands in Melyapalam, while his sons Kulandhaisamy and Kuttisamy were primarily engaged in farming and agricultural activities. Theerthagiri, along with his brothers Thambi and Kilothar, oversaw the administration and security of the villages under their family's jurisdiction.

The brothers were well-trained in martial arts, archery, and horse riding, and they actively participated in village panchayats, gaining experience in resolving land and family disputes, which would later shape Theerthagiri’s leadership and sense of justice.

Muhammad Ali, the diwan of Mysore kingdom under Hyder Ali, who oversaw the tax collection, followed rather unfair practices, at times even grabbing lands. It was during one such encounter, that Theerthagiri and his brother confronted him between Sennimalai and Shivanmalai , recovering the taxes he had forcibly collected.

On being threatened of severe consequences for the defiance, Theerthagiri shot back, saying could govern itself well, and would not accept the rule of Hyder Ali, proclaiming defiantly.

*"I am Chinnamalai who reigns between Sennimalai and Shivanmalai".*

Predictably, Hyder Ali retaliated by dispatching an army to Kongu to crush Chinnamalai’s defiance. However, he decisively routed the Mysore forces on the banks of the Noyyal River, dealing a humiliating blow.

An enraged Muhammad Ali vowed vengeance, but even as Chinnamalai began strengthening his forces in anticipation of renewed conflict, the threat never fully materialized.

Hyder Ali became preoccupied with larger conflicts—against the Nizam of Hyderabad, the British, and the Marathas—which diverted his attention away from Kongu Nadu, giving Chinnamalai crucial time to consolidate his power.

With Tipu Sultan’s rise to power in 1782, the Kingdom of Mysore adopted an even more aggressive stance against the British. Seeking to strengthen his resistance, Tipu called upon the support of regional leaders, including those from Kongu Nadu. Chinnamalai, along with his brothers and trusted commanders Velappan and Karuppan, responded to the call, leading the Kongu regiment as part of the Mysore Army.

They played an active role in both the Third and Fourth Anglo-Mysore Wars, standing firmly against British expansion.However, with the fall of Srirangapatnam and Tipu Sultan’s death in 1799, the resistance collapsed.

Chinnamalai and Karuppan returned to Kongu, regrouping in their homeland. Velappan, unfortunately, was captured by the British and later turned collaborator, serving as their agent—an act that would have serious implications in the years to come.

Having received French military training during his time under Tipu Sultan, Chinnamalai returned to Kongu with renewed determination. He constructed a fortress at Odaanilai, where he settled with his loyal troops, patiently waiting for the right moment to strike against the British.

Seeking to build a broader resistance, he reached out to the rulers of Malabar and Salem, hoping to forge a wider alliance against colonial domination.

Alarmed by his growing influence and military preparedness, the British attempted to neutralize him diplomatically, offering him a pact with promises of favors and protection, in exchange for acknowledging their sovereignty.

Chinnamalai firmly refused. He was well aware that signing such a pact would mean surrendering his people’s autonomy and betraying the spirit of resistance. His defiance infuriated the British.

That a man with no formal title, not a king or a zamindar, could openly reject their authority—when even established rajahs had bowed to them—was a direct affront to their imperial arrogance.

In 1801, the British dispatched a contingent under Colonel Maxwell to crush Chinnamalai’s growing resistance. However, having received advance intelligence of the attack, Chinnamalai ambushed the British on the banks of the Noyyal River, inflicting a decisive defeat.

Undeterred, the British launched a second assault in 1802, laying siege to the Odaanilai Fort. But once again, they were routed. Chinnamalai’s forces not only repelled the siege but dealt a fatal blow—Colonel Maxwell was captured and beheaded, sending shockwaves through the British command.

Though enraged by the repeated defeats, the British bided their time, waiting for the perfect opportunity. It came three years later in 1805, when Chinnamalai and most of his army were away, attending the Arasalur Amman Temple festivities.

Recognizing this rare vulnerability, they launched a massive offensive under General Harris, the very commander who had led the final campaign against Tipu Sultan.

However, Chinnamalai received timely intelligence of the planned ambush. In a brilliant tactical move, he remained at the fort with the core of his army, lying in wait.

As Harris’s troops approached, Chinnamalai launched a surprise counterattack, including the use of hand grenades, a rare weapon at the time. Caught off guard, General Harris was forced to retreat, marking yet another stunning reversal for the British.

After the fall of Odaanilai, Chinnamalai and his brothers went into exile, taking refuge in the hilly forests of Karumalai, near Palani. Living in disguise, they would occasionally venture into nearby towns, always wary of British patrols and informants.

They were sheltered by Nallappan, a local cook, who appeared to be a sympathizer but was secretly aligned with the British. Seizing the opportunity for reward and favor, Nallappan betrayed them, providing the British with precise information about their location.

One night, while Chinnamalai and his brothers were having dinner, British troops, acting on Nallappan’s signal, stormed the house, taking them by surprise.

Enraged by the betrayal, a furious Chinnamalai is said to have strangled Nallappan to death with his bare hands before being captured by the British, along with his brothers and loyal commander Karuppan.

They were taken to the Sangagiri Fort, where they were tried before a four-member British tribunal. The tribunal demanded that Chinnamalai pay taxes and accept British sovereignty—a final attempt to subdue the proud rebel through humiliation.

Chinnamalai refused defiantly, rejecting both their authority and their terms. As a result, he was sentenced to death, along with his brothers and Karuppan.

On July 31, 1805, Dheeran Chinnamalai, his brothers, and his faithful commander were hanged, bringing an end to the Kongu revolt. Though the rebellion was crushed, his defiance and sacrifice became legend, inspiring generations of freedom fighters in Tamil Nadu and beyond.
